name: contributing
context: Contribution guide — fork, branch, validate, and open a GitHub PR to add or edit Dojo skills
info: Step-by-step guide for contributing to the Dojo registry through GitHub pull requests. Covers forking the repo, creating a feature branch, scaffolding manifests, filling rich knowledge fields, validating against the schema, smoke-testing against the local registry server, writing good commit messages, opening a PR, and what to expect from review.
triggers: how do i contribute to dojo
how do i publish a dojo skill
how do i validate a dojo manifest
what is the dojo contribution flow
how do i open a pr for dojo
how to add a new skill to dojo
how to add a new ecosystem to dojo
what does dojo review look for
body:
# Contributing to Dojo

All contributions to the Dojo registry go through GitHub pull requests. There is no separate publish command — merging your PR **is** publishing.

## Workflow at a glance

1. **Fork** — `gh repo fork keychain-inc/dojo --clone`
2. **Branch** — `git checkout -b feat/<ecosystem>/<skill-name>`
3. **Scaffold** — `dojo init <name> --type <type> --parent <parent-uri>`
4. **Author** — fill identity, decision, knowledge, and (if executable) script fields
5. **Validate** — `dojo validate ./skill.json`
6. **Smoke-test** — `npm run dev` in `server/`, then hit the API
7. **Commit** — conventional-commit message, one manifest per commit
8. **Push & PR** — `git push origin feat/...` then `gh pr create --fill`
9. **Review** — maintainers check validity, quality, and coverage
10. **Merge** — squash-merge lands the node into the live registry

## Concrete example: adding a Redis pub/sub guide

Let's walk through adding a knowledge node `redis/pubsub-patterns` as a `context` node under the existing `redis` ecosystem:

```bash
# 1. Fork and clone
gh repo fork keychain-inc/dojo --clone
cd dojo

# 2. Branch
git checkout -b feat/redis/pubsub-patterns

# 3. Scaffold
mkdir -p examples/redis/pubsub-patterns
dojo init pubsub-patterns --type context --parent redis
# creates examples/redis/pubsub-patterns/skill.json

# 4. Author — edit the manifest to add body, sections, etc.
# (see below for what to fill in)

# 5. Validate
dojo validate examples/redis/pubsub-patterns/skill.json

# 6. Smoke-test
cd server && npm run dev &
curl localhost:3000/v1/tree/redis | jq .
curl localhost:3000/v1/learn/redis/pubsub-patterns | jq .
curl 'localhost:3000/v1/search?q=pubsub+patterns' | jq .

# 7. Commit
git add examples/redis/pubsub-patterns/
git commit -m 'feat(redis): add pubsub-patterns context node'

# 8. Push & PR
git push origin feat/redis/pubsub-patterns
gh pr create --fill
```

See [[dojo/spec]] for hierarchy rules and [[dojo/architecture]] for the runtime model.

sections.title: Fork and branch
sections.body:
Fork `keychain-inc/dojo` via the GitHub UI or CLI:

```bash
gh repo fork keychain-inc/dojo --clone
cd dojo
```

Create a feature branch named `feat/<ecosystem>/<skill-name>` — for example `feat/ethereum/erc4626` or `feat/redis/pubsub-patterns`. Keep one skill per branch unless the skills are tightly coupled (e.g. a standard and its first child skill).

sections.title: Scaffold a manifest
sections.body:
Run `dojo init` to generate a skeleton:

```bash
dojo init my-skill --type skill --parent my-ecosystem/my-standard
```

This creates `examples/<parent-path>/my-skill/skill.json` pre-filled with `name`, `version`, `uri`, `type`, and `parent`. You fill in the rest.

For a new **ecosystem**, create the directory and manifest manually:
```bash
mkdir -p examples/my-eco
cat > examples/my-eco/skill.json << 'EOF'
{"name": "my-eco", "version": "1.0.0", "uri": "my-eco",
 "type": "ecosystem", "parent": null,
 "context": "...", "info": "...", "tags": ["..."]}
EOF
```

sections.title: Authoring: identity and decision fields
sections.body:
Start with the fields agents use for routing:

- `context` — the single most important field. Write it like a commit subject: short (max 200 chars), specific, decisive. An agent reads this to decide "is this the right skill?" Bad: `"Does stuff with tokens"`. Good: `"Transfer ERC-20 tokens between addresses on any EVM chain"`.
- `info` — the first paragraph of the README. Explain when to use this skill, what it covers, and what it doesn't.
- `tags` — lowercase, at least one. Think about what someone would search for.
- `triggers` — natural-language phrases that should activate this skill. Write 3-7 phrases people actually say: `"send tokens"`, `"transfer ERC-20"`, `"move my USDC"`.

sections.title: Authoring: rich knowledge fields
sections.body:
For any node that teaches or explains (not just executes), fill in:

- `aliases` — alternate names people use. `["fungible token", "ERC-20", "token standard"]`
- `body` — long-form markdown. Use `[[uri]]` wiki-links to other nodes. Include code examples, tables, diagrams.
- `sections` — break the body into addressable chunks with `{id, title, body, tags}`. Each section gets its own search tags.
- `links` — directed outgoing navigation: `{uri, context}`. Link to prerequisites and related skills.
- `related` — semantic ties: `{uri, relation, note}`. Use `prerequisite`, `alternative`, `similar`, `see-also`, etc.
- `content_type` — categorize: `reference`, `guide`, `explainer`, `comparison`, `changelog`, `faq`, `glossary`.
- `frontmatter` — editorial metadata: `audience`, `estimated_reading_time`, `confidence`.

A thin manifest will validate but won't teach agents much. Compare [[ethereum/erc20]] (rich) with any bare-minimum skill.

sections.title: Authoring: scripts and execution
sections.body:
For executable nodes (`skill` and `sub` types), add:

- `scripts` — array of script objects with `id`, `name`, `lang`, and either `entry` (file path) or `inline` (source code). Add `env` for environment variables and `packages` for dependencies.
- `schema` — `{input, output}` as JSON Schema objects. Agents use these to validate arguments and parse results.
- `depends` — cross-skill dependencies with `{uri, version, optional, reason}`.

Always test scripts locally before committing. If your skill needs secrets, document them in `env` with `secret: true` and `description`.

sections.title: Validate
sections.body:
Run `dojo validate ./skill.json` to check against the JSON Schema at `schema/skill.schema.json`.

Common failures:
- `context` exceeds 200 characters — tighten the wording
- Invalid `type`/`parent` combination — check [[dojo/spec#hierarchy]]
- Missing required fields — `name`, `version`, `uri`, `type`, `context`, `info`, `tags`
- Bad URI pattern — must be lowercase, slash-separated, no special chars
- Invalid `content_type` — must be one of: `reference`, `guide`, `explainer`, `comparison`, `changelog`, `faq`, `glossary`
- Bad tag format — must match `^[a-z0-9-]+$`

Fix all validation errors before committing.

sections.title: Local registry smoke test
sections.body:
Start the registry server locally and verify your node appears correctly:

```bash
cd server && npm run dev &

# Check tree includes your node
curl localhost:3000/v1/tree/<ecosystem> | jq '.children[] | .uri'

# Check search finds your node
curl 'localhost:3000/v1/search?q=<keyword>' | jq '.[].uri'

# Check knowledge renders
curl localhost:3000/v1/learn/<your-uri> | jq '.body, .sections'

# Check backlinks work (if you linked to other nodes)
curl localhost:3000/v1/backlinks/<target-uri> | jq '.[].uri'
```

This catches: bad parents (node doesn't appear in tree), missing index coverage (node doesn't appear in search), thin content (learn endpoint returns empty body), and broken links.

sections.title: Commit conventions
sections.body:
Use conventional commits with the ecosystem as scope:

- `feat(ethereum): add erc4626 vault skill` — new node
- `fix(dojo/spec): correct parent rule wording` — content fix
- `docs(dojo): expand architecture sections` — knowledge improvement
- `refactor(aws/lambda): restructure sub-skills` — reorganization

One logical commit per manifest. If you're fixing up earlier commits in the branch, squash them: `git rebase -i` before requesting review.

sections.title: Open a pull request
sections.body:
Push your branch and open a PR against `main`:

```bash
git push origin feat/<ecosystem>/<skill-name>
gh pr create --fill
```

The PR description should include:
- **What** — which ecosystem/skill you're adding or changing
- **Why** — motivation or use case
- **Checklist:**
  - [ ] `dojo validate` passes
  - [ ] Local smoke test passes (tree, search, learn)
  - [ ] `body`/`sections` filled for knowledge nodes
  - [ ] `scripts` tested for executable nodes
  - [ ] `links`/`related` URIs point to existing nodes

PRs that fail validation or have empty knowledge fields for `context`-type nodes will be sent back for revision.

sections.title: Review criteria
sections.body:
Maintainers evaluate PRs on:

1. **Schema validity** — `dojo validate` must pass
2. **Parent correctness** — parent URI exists and the type combo is valid per [[dojo/spec#hierarchy]]
3. **Context quality** — `context` is concise, specific, and helps an agent decide whether to use this node
4. **Content depth** — `body` and `sections` teach something meaningful for knowledge nodes; scripts are tested for executable nodes
5. **Search coverage** — `aliases` and `tags` help discovery; `triggers` use natural phrasing
6. **Link integrity** — `links` and `related` URIs point to existing nodes with correct `relation` types
7. **Commit hygiene** — conventional commits, squashed fixups, clean history

Reviewers may suggest richer content, better `context` wording, or additional cross-links. This is collaboration, not gatekeeping.

sections.title: After merge
sections.body:
Once your PR is squash-merged into `main`, the registry server picks up the new manifest on next deploy. There is no separate publish step.

Verify in production:
```bash
curl https://dojo.dev/v1/learn/<your-uri> | jq '.body' | head
curl 'https://dojo.dev/v1/search?q=<keyword>' | jq '.[].uri'
```

If your node doesn't appear, check that the deploy completed and that the server re-indexed. See [[dojo/architecture#search-index]].

sections.title: Troubleshooting contributions
sections.body:
**PR fails CI.** Check `dojo validate` output. Common issues: `context` too long, missing required fields, bad URI pattern, invalid `content_type`.

**Node doesn't appear in local search.** Make sure `tags`, `aliases`, or `body` contain your search terms. The search index only covers populated fields.

**"parent not found" error.** The parent URI must match an existing node in `examples/`. If you're adding a new ecosystem, the ecosystem's own `skill.json` must exist first.

**Merge conflicts.** Rebase onto `main`: `git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main`. Manifest files rarely conflict since each skill lives in its own directory.

**Reviewer asks for richer content.** Add `body`, `sections`, `aliases`. Compare your manifest against [[ethereum/erc20]] or [[dojo/spec]] for the level of detail expected.
